draft.introduction: The Yoga Vāsiṣṭha is a philosophical dialogue in which Vasishtha teaches Prince Rama that the world is a projection of consciousness. Reality is nothing but awareness dreaming itself.

translation.text: Rama said: “O great sage, I see this world and it appears real, yet wise men say it is unreal. My mind is tormented by this contradiction.” Vasishtha replied: “This world appearance is like a dream. It persists as long as one does not inquire into its nature. Upon inquiry, it vanishes like a dream upon waking.”
translation.original_text: rāma uvāca | idaṃ jagat satīvābhāti | vasiṣṭha uvāca | svapnavat jagad ābhāti

translation.text: Consciousness alone is the substance of all experience. What appears as the world is consciousness modifying itself, as a dreamer modifies dream-space. There is no creation and no destruction—only the infinite play of awareness.
translation.original_text: cit eva jagad-ākāraṃ nāsti bhūtaṃ na bhāvi ca

translator_notes.note: The Yoga Vāsiṣṭha’s dream-world metaphor is the most sustained idealist argument in Indian philosophy. It influenced śaṅkara’s Advaita and prefigures phenomenological arguments: the world is not denied but recognized as consciousness-dependent.
